video_title: The Birth of America’s Nuclear Power Industry w/ James Krellenstein
gist: James Krellenstein argues the 'golden age' of cheap US nuclear was a myth engineered by Westinghouse and GE, who deliberately sold ~10 turnkey fixed-price plants below cost (~$1B 1960s / ~$12B inflation-adjusted in losses) to break open utility order books — a one-time loss-leader play that won them lasting NSSS and fuel-market dominance but is not actually repeatable evidence that nuclear beat coal on true build cost.

insights.claim: Westinghouse and GE deliberately sold roughly 10-12 turnkey nuclear plants at fixed prices far below build cost, taking about $1B in 1960s dollars (~$10-12B inflation-adjusted) in combined losses, as an industrial-policy move to jumpstart utility ordering.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Reframes the cheap-nuclear narrative: early low utility prices reflected vendor subsidy, not true cost — caution against citing 1960s plant prices as a buildable cost benchmark.
insights.quote: There was a deliberate choice between both at Skenctity and Pittsburgh at this time at Westinghouse and GE to basically make take very very large losses on those plants to basically set off a massive amount of ordering

insights.claim: GE's Oyster Creek (NJ) turnkey order in December 1963 priced at $132/kW was sold to Jersey Central Power as cheaper than coal for that site, becoming the catalyst that opened utility order flow on purely economic grounds.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: The single inflection point of the US nuclear buildout was a marketing-priced contract, not a cost breakthrough — the 'beat coal' claim originates here.
insights.quote: they basically deliver it to Jersey Central Power as the turnkey project at a fixed price in 1963 of $132 a kilowatt. And for that site that was going to be cheaper than what a coal fired plant was going to cost

insights.claim: On individual turnkey plants the utility price was often less than the vendor's loss: Oyster Creek sold for ~$90M vs ~$170M GE cost; Quad Cities ~$200M vs ~$448M; Point Beach ~$145M vs ~$329M Westinghouse cost (a ~$184M loss).
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Quantifies the gap between sticker price and true build cost — the empirical basis for distrusting 'historically cheap' nuclear cost figures.
insights.quote: Oyster Creek costs $90 million... It estimated GE cost $170 million to build it... Point Beach was paid for about $145 million. It likely cost Westinghouse $329 million to build.

insights.claim: GE publicly abandoned turnkey nuclear construction in summer 1966 and Westinghouse quietly followed, and neither company ever returned to that fixed-price loss-leader model.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Signals the turnkey subsidy was explicitly one-shot; expecting incumbents to repeat it today is unrealistic.
insights.quote: in basically summer of ' 66, General Electric announces they're no longer building nuclear power plants turnkey. And although Westinghouse doesn't announce it publicly, basically uh Westinghouse also stops building turnkey.

insights.claim: Bechtel entered as the lead EPC offering firm-fixed-price wrapped nuclear contracts (e.g. Turkey Point 3 and 4 in Florida), and the turnkey gamble cemented GE, Westinghouse and Bechtel as the dominant NSSS, fuel and EPC players for decades.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: The loss-leader spend bought durable oligopoly position — frames why incumbents tolerated huge near-term losses for long-term market control.

insights.claim: The turnkey losses were a deliberately expensive but highly profitable long-term investment, as GE and Westinghouse recouped them many times over by dominating the US nuclear steam supply system and nuclear fuel markets through 2026.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Models the strategic logic for any new entrant: tolerate first-of-a-kind losses to capture a structurally concentrated downstream market.
insights.quote: General Electric and Westinghouse made that many, many times over in their ability to dominate the US nuclear steam steam supply system market and the US nuclear fuel market. So, it was a really good long-term investment

insights.claim: Early 'cheap' plants like Point Beach, Oyster Creek, Dresden and Monticello were cheap to the utility but not actually cheap to build, contradicting the Jack Devanney / Brett Kugelmass / Alex Epstein narrative that early nuclear achieved parity with coal.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Directly disputes a popular pro-nuclear cost thesis; investors should discount 'just rebuild the cheap Gen-2 plants' arguments that rest on subsidized historical prices.
insights.quote: they weren't actually cheap to build necessarily or at least they weren't as cheap as the price implies

insights.claim: Before Oyster Creek, utilities (as developer-builders) consistently found nuclear plants like Connecticut Yankee and San Onofre 1 were not competitive with coal even with AEC subsidies, so they refused to order them.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Establishes that genuine pre-turnkey nuclear economics lost to coal — the baseline against which the turnkey 'win' was manufactured.
insights.quote: the utilities just refused to actually order the plants because they just could not figure out even with an AEC subsidy how to make these economical

insights.claim: American Electric Power CEO Philip Sporn argued in 1962 that nuclear would always cost 50-100% more than coal, and after Oyster Creek some utilities openly called the turnkey plants loss leaders and predicted reactors would later become more expensive.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Contemporary insiders flagged the subsidy in real time and correctly forecast future cost escalation — a documented bear case that proved right.
insights.quote: the Oyster Creek announcement comes out in December of 62... some of the utilities literally suggest, hey, these look like loss leaders in some ways

insights.claim: Peach Bottom 2 and 3 (1,000+ MW BWRs, now uprated to ~1,400 MW/unit) were built roughly an order of magnitude cheaper per kW than the 40-48 MW Peach Bottom 1 high-temperature gas reactor at the same site, with no subsidies.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that nuclear economics are scale-driven; supports 'go big' over small modular designs on a pure cost-per-kW basis.
insights.quote: Peach Bottom two and Peach Bottom three which were big, you know, thousand megawatt plus boiling water reactors roughly at an order of magnitude lower cost per kilowatt compared to what Peach Bottom unit number one cost

insights.claim: General Atomics scaled its HTGR from 48 MW Peach Bottom to 330 MW Fort St. Vrain and, despite Fort St. Vrain being first-of-a-kind, built it roughly threefold cheaper per kW purely through scale-up.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Demonstrates scale beats first-of-a-kind cost penalties — a reusable lever for evaluating reactor cost claims.
insights.quote: Fort St. Vrain as the scaled up version of this at 330 megawatts electrical... built roughly threefold cheaper per kilowatt electrical than Peach Bottom

insights.claim: Plants built at large scale into the early 1970s achieved very low capital costs — Peach Bottom 2/3 ~$395/kW (1982 terms) and Palisades ~$393/kW — making them highly competitive with coal once coal's ongoing fuel and handling costs are counted.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Shows large unsubsidized plants genuinely could rival coal, distinguishing real scale economics from the turnkey subsidy illusion.
insights.quote: Peach Bottom 2 and three... was basically built at a pretty extraordinary cost of about $395 in 1982 per kilowatt electric. Palisades, right, comes in at $393.

insights.claim: It was not clear in the mid-1950s that light water reactors would dominate; the AEC's Power Reactor Demonstration Program funded a dozen-plus diverse designs (sodium fast, helium HTGR, heavy water, organic-moderated, graphite-moderated) and the first global power reactors were mostly non-LWR.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Reactor technology winners were path-dependent and non-obvious — useful prior for judging today's advanced-reactor design bets.
insights.quote: LWRs were not the the necessarily the dominant choice that if you were looking at the situation 1954 1955 you would have guessed

insights.claim: Non-light-water PRDP demonstrators performed so poorly that most were not operated after their demo period: Fermi 1 ran so little it needed an oil boiler for its turbine, and Hallam was built from day one with a coal boiler sharing the turbine in anticipation of poor nuclear availability.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Historical operational failure of exotic reactors is a cautionary data point for non-LWR advanced reactor capacity-factor risk.
insights.quote: Fermy 1 being the famous example where it was so poor in operation that... they built an oil boiler to utilize the turbine just cuz the reactor was off offline so much

insights.claim: Babcock & Wilcox and Combustion Engineering were the only US firms (in Indiana and Chattanooga, TN) with the heavy-forging capability to make reactor pressure vessels for both GE and Westinghouse, who never built that capability internally.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Heavy-forging RPV capacity was a structural supply-chain chokepoint even at the industry's peak — relevant to today's nuclear supply-chain bottlenecks.
insights.quote: Only those two companies in Indiana in the case of BMW and in Chattanooga, Tennessee in the case of Combustion Engineering had the heavy forging capabilities to manufacture the RPVs

insights.claim: 1960s GE and Westinghouse were among the world's strongest corporations — effectively the 'magnificent seven' of their day — spanning power generation equipment, transmission infrastructure, appliances, locomotives, jet engines, broadcasting and nuclear weapons/navy contracting, giving them the balance sheet to absorb turnkey losses.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Explains who could bankroll a loss-leader nuclear push then, and implies today no comparable private balance sheet exists to repeat it.
insights.quote: They were the magnificent seven.

insights.claim: In 1960 the US had only about 200 MW of operating nuclear capacity, and the nuclear industry at that point was almost entirely defense-related (weapons production and the nuclear navy), not civilian power.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Sets the tiny civilian baseline from which the buildout exploded — context for how extraordinary the 1962-76 growth was.
insights.quote: In 1960, there's about 200 megawatts electrical of nuclear power plants operating in the US.

insights.claim: The US nuclear fleet roughly doubled capacity every two years between 1962 and 1976, building or starting essentially the entire (still world's largest) fleet in ~14 years — described as possibly the fastest sustained growth of any major US industry before the AI/Silicon Valley booms.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Historical proof that explosive nuclear scale-up is possible given the right catalyst — directly analogized to today's AI-driven power demand.
insights.quote: between 1962 and 19776, the US nuclear power industry basically doubled its capacity approximately every 2 years

insights.claim: Coal prices nearly doubled between 1966 and 1972 (from roughly $4.50 to ~$7-8 a short ton), and Hubbert-peak fears of running out of fossil fuels plus relentless postwar load-growth extrapolation drove utilities to diversify into nuclear.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Demand-side drivers (rising coal, scarcity fear, load-growth extrapolation) — a framework Keefer flags as extrapolation bias relevant to today's forecasts.
insights.quote: between 1966, right, to 1972, coal prices nearly double

insights.claim: Between 1966 and 1974 the inflation-adjusted cost of a coal plant roughly doubled while a nuclear plant rose about fivefold, hitting nuclear disproportionately because it is so capital-intensive.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Quantifies why nuclear economics collapsed — broad power-plant cost inflation amplified by nuclear's capital intensity, beyond just nuclear-specific regulation.
insights.quote: between 1966 and 1974 on average a the cost of a coal power plant doubles on an inflation adjusted basis. A nuclear plant by the way increases by about on average about fivefold in that time.

insights.claim: Early-1970s regulatory changes — Calvert Cliffs / NEPA compliance, the GMBBO letter on pipe-whip and high-energy line breaks, and retrofits after the 1975 Browns Ferry fire and ATWS event — materially raised nuclear costs, but slowing load growth and resulting cancellations were the bigger driver of the bust.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Separates regulatory cost from the demand collapse — the late-70s cancellation wave was driven as much by falling load growth as by regulation.
insights.quote: the main thing that's generally happening here is that load growth is generally decline or is starting to really slow down... we start seeing huge numbers of cancellations occurring in the in the late 70s and early 80s

insights.claim: Cost-plus rate-base regulation let utilities pass prudent capital costs plus a guaranteed profit margin to ratepayers, and since allowed returns applied to capex (not fuel/opex), capital-heavy, low-opex nuclear plants became unusually attractive to utility P&Ls.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Explains the post-1966 ordering frenzy via regulated-utility incentives — a reusable lens for why capital-intensive assets thrive under cost-of-service regulation.
insights.quote: you were allowed to profit on capital costs and a nuclear power plant is a very capital inensive project and a very very low operating cost expenses. So from the utilities own profit and loss perspective a nuclear power plant became very very attractive

insights.claim: Krellenstein argues today's situation is better than the 1960s because first-of-a-kind Gen-3 reactors (e.g. Vogtle) are now built and operating so the design is proven, but reigniting a buildout still requires recreating a 'turnkey era' with a balance sheet willing to guarantee firm fixed prices.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Identifies the core bottleneck for a nuclear renaissance as risk-bearing capital, not technology — watch who provides firm-fixed-price guarantees.
insights.quote: if we're going to basically figure out how to do this again, we're going to have to basically create another turnkey era

insights.claim: Because no private firm will repeat GE/Westinghouse's loss-leader model, Krellenstein implies the firm-fixed-price risk-bearer for new nuclear must be government (US or, e.g., Japanese government on behalf of the US) or another balance sheet large enough to guarantee cost and monetize the next five-to-ten units.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Points investors toward government-backed or sovereign financing structures as the likely enabler of any serious US nuclear scale-up.
insights.quote: It sounds like it's got to be government or it's got to be the Japanese government on behalf of the US government. It's got to be either it's going to be some sort of person with a balance sheet that's large enough

insights.claim: Recent attempts to bear fixed-price nuclear risk ended badly: Westinghouse went effectively bankrupt over Vogtle with Shaw, and Areva similarly faltered on the EPRs and no longer exists, despite delivering benefits to ratepayers.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Modern firm-fixed-price nuclear bets have bankrupted their backers — a sobering precedent for any vendor or financier weighing the same structure.
insights.quote: Westinghouse, to their credit, I think in in the Vogal situation did try to do that and ultimately went went bankrupt a little bit trying to try do that with with Shaw

insights.claim: Krellenstein steelmans the counter-thesis that the turnkey history is an indictment of large light-water reactors and that the path forward is smaller advanced/micro reactors needing less capital per unit — but notes the irony that small reactors are exactly where the first turnkey era started and failed.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Flags the central unresolved debate (go-big LWR vs small modular) and warns SMR optimism repeats a historically failed bet — key risk for SMR investors.
insights.quote: the problem there is the irony is is actually that's where we started in the first turnkey era and we abandoned that approach because it just so did not work

insights.claim: Krellenstein notes the AP1000 has roughly 80% less safety-related piping, 85% less control cable and 50% fewer valves than earlier designs, framing the open question of why standardized Gen-3+ plants still can't undercut coal.
insights.speaker: James Krellenstein
insights.so_what: Design simplification alone hasn't delivered sub-coal cost, reinforcing that the binding constraint is execution/financing risk rather than plant complexity.
insights.quote: the AP1000 has 80% less safety related piping, 85% less control cable, 50% less valves
